prompt: EnergyPlus and OpenStudio workflows commonly exchange data through:

options.text: IDF/OSM building descriptions, schedules, and simulation output CSV/SQL reports
Handwritten field notes only with no digital files
Structural Revit families without thermal properties
Only PDF utility bills embedded as images

explanation: BEMP candidates should understand file types and data flow in common simulation stacks.

distractorExplanations.b: Modern workflows rely on structured input and output files.
distractorExplanations.c: Thermal models need material and construction definitions.
distractorExplanations.d: Bills inform calibration; they are not simulation input geometry.

prompt: When a model shows lower winter gas use than measured, a modeler should first check:

options.text: Heating setpoints, boiler efficiency curves, and whether non-HVAC gas end uses are included in meter data
Whether the weather file contains summer months only
Only the number of simulation timesteps per hour
Deleting all infiltration to force a match

explanation: Meter normalization must separate HVAC from process loads before blaming the heating model.

distractorExplanations.b: TMY/EPW files include full annual weather.
distractorExplanations.c: Timestep affects accuracy but non-HVAC gas is a common mismatch cause.
distractorExplanations.d: Arbitrary input deletion undermines model integrity.

prompt: Daylighting controls in models reduce lighting energy by:

options.text: Dimming or switching electric lighting based on simulated daylight availability and control type
Increasing LPD when sun is present
Disabling all lighting schedules year-round
Removing all windows from the model

explanation: Photosensor or timed daylighting algorithms modulate lighting power during occupied hours.

distractorExplanations.b: Daylighting controls reduce electric lighting when daylight is ample.
distractorExplanations.c: Schedules still define occupied hours; daylighting adjusts power within them.
distractorExplanations.d: Windows enable daylighting control savings.

prompt: Source energy factors in reporting convert site energy to:

options.text: Primary fuel use accounting for generation, transmission, and distribution losses
Only the building's water consumption in gallons
Occupant satisfaction survey scores
Structural beam weights in pounds

explanation: LEED and ASHRAE publications use source factors to compare environmental impact across fuels.

distractorExplanations.b: Source energy addresses fuel upstream of the site meter.
distractorExplanations.c: Source energy is a physical energy metric.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural quantities are unrelated to source energy.

prompt: A revolving door vs swing door selection affects models primarily through:

options.text: Infiltration and air exchange assumptions at the building entrance
Chiller refrigerant type selection
Transformer impedance on the electrical service
Fire pump flow requirements exclusively

explanation: Entrance air leakage can be material in high-traffic buildings during heating/cooling seasons.

distractorExplanations.b: Door type influences air leakage, not refrigerant chemistry.
distractorExplanations.c: Electrical distribution is independent of door configuration.
distractorExplanations.d: Fire protection flows are governed by code hydraulics, not door type.

prompt: Automated quality checks should flag if:

options.text: Simulated peak demand occurs with all lights and equipment off while cooling is zero
The model includes at least one thermal zone
Weather data includes wind speed
An annual simulation has 8,760 hours

explanation: Nonsensical load combinations indicate schedule or equipment input errors.

distractorExplanations.b: Having zones is expected, not an error by itself.
distractorExplanations.c: Wind is a normal weather variable.
distractorExplanations.d: 8,760 hours is standard for annual runs.

prompt: For a proposed LEED v4 BD+C Optimize Energy Performance credit, the simulation must:

options.text: Follow ASHRAE 90.1 Appendix G and LEED modeling rules including baseline cost methodology
Use only prescriptive envelope tables with no simulation
Ignore all renewable generation on site
Compare only water use intensity between buildings

explanation: LEED EA credits reference Appendix G modeling protocols and supplemental LEED guidance.

distractorExplanations.b: The credit path requires whole-building performance modeling.
distractorExplanations.c: LEED rules define how renewables are treated; they are not ignored by default.
distractorExplanations.d: The credit is about energy performance improvement.

prompt: Thermal mass effects in lightweight vs heavyweight construction influence:

options.text: Peak load timing and diurnal load shifting due to heat storage in opaque assemblies
Only plumbing fixture flow rates
Elevator motor horsepower exclusively
Refrigerant global warming potential only

explanation: High-mass walls delay and attenuate conduction peaks compared with lightweight framing.

distractorExplanations.b: Thermal mass affects HVAC load profiles.
distractorExplanations.c: Elevator loads are separate from envelope mass effects.
distractorExplanations.d: GWP is a refrigerant property, not a mass effect.

prompt: When merging as-built changes late in design, the modeler should:

options.text: Update geometry, schedules, and efficiencies with version notes and re-run calibration checks
Leave outdated inputs to preserve prior savings claims without documentation
Delete the baseline case to avoid rework
Switch to a different climate zone arbitrarily

explanation: Change control prevents silent drift between reported and actual design conditions.

distractorExplanations.b: As-built changes require model updates and transparent revision logs.
distractorExplanations.c: Baseline and proposed cases both need consistency after changes.
distractorExplanations.d: Climate zone is fixed by project location.

prompt: Cogeneration or CHP modeled on site should account for:

options.text: Fuel input, electric output, recovered heat utilization, and parallel vs series configuration
Only nameplate electric kW with no heat recovery path
Infinite free natural gas regardless of runtime
Treating CHP as grid purchases only

explanation: CHP dispatch logic determines whether savings accrue to heating, electricity, or both.

distractorExplanations.b: CHP efficiency depends on useful heat recovery.
distractorExplanations.c: Fuel consumption must be simulated based on load dispatch.
distractorExplanations.d: On-site generation offsets purchased electricity and fuel.

prompt: An ideal air system assumption in early schematic models:

options.text: Simplifies HVAC distribution but may misrepresent fan energy and zone humidity control
Perfectly matches all VRF refrigerant circuits
Eliminates the need for any ventilation airflow
Guarantees zero simultaneous heating and cooling

explanation: Modelers trade early simplicity for later detailed system representation as design advances.

distractorExplanations.b: Ideal air systems do not model refrigerant distribution detail.
distractorExplanations.c: Ventilation must still be specified under ideal air assumptions.
distractorExplanations.d: Ideal air can still show reheat penalties if minimums are modeled.

prompt: Reporting peak coincident demand for utility cost requires:

options.text: Aligning simulated demand intervals with tariff demand ratchet and billing window rules
Using annual average kW as the peak for all months
Ignoring demand charges entirely in cost reports
Multiplying total kWh by occupancy count

explanation: Cost models must mirror utility rate structures including demand ratchets and TOU energy charges.

distractorExplanations.b: Tariffs bill based on interval peak demand, not annual averages.
distractorExplanations.c: Demand charges often drive ECM payback for large buildings.
distractorExplanations.d: Demand is a power peak metric, not kWh per person.

prompt: Natural ventilation modeling requires defining:

options.text: Opening schedules, wind pressure coefficients, and cross-ventilation or stack effect pathways
Only mechanical cooling capacity with sealed windows
Refrigerant charge in pounds per ton only
Structural weld inspection intervals

explanation: Hybrid ventilation models couple airflow networks or simplified opening rules with HVAC operation.

distractorExplanations.b: Natural ventilation needs operable opening logic.
distractorExplanations.c: Refrigerant charge applies to mechanical cooling systems.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural inspection is unrelated to ventilation modeling.

prompt: A BEMP professional interpreting ECM savings should disclose:

options.text: Interactive effects, measurement uncertainty, and operational assumptions affecting persistence
Only the largest single ECM number without context
Guaranteed savings with no O&M requirements
That calibration eliminates all future weather risk

explanation: Professional practice includes transparent limits of modeled savings projections.

distractorExplanations.b: Stacked ECMs may interact; uncertainty affects investment decisions.
distractorExplanations.c: Persistence depends on continued proper operation.
distractorExplanations.d: Future weather and occupancy differ from the simulation year.

prompt: Heat pump modeling in cold climates must address:

options.text: Auxiliary heat staging, defrost cycles, and capacity degradation at low outdoor temperatures
Only constant COP of 10 at all temperatures
Excluding backup heat because heat pumps never need it
Modeling heat pumps as electric resistance only

explanation: Accurate cold-climate heat pump inputs prevent overstated heating savings.

distractorExplanations.b: COP varies with outdoor temperature and defrost.
distractorExplanations.c: Many commercial heat pumps use supplemental heat in cold weather.
distractorExplanations.d: That would ignore heat pump performance curves.

prompt: The purpose of ASHRAE Standard 140 (best practices for software evaluation) is to:

options.text: Provide comparative test cases so simulation engines can be validated against analytical solutions
Set minimum passing scores for BEMP candidates
Define kitchen exhaust hood capture velocity only
Replace all building energy codes nationally

explanation: Standard 140 test suites help ensure simulation tools produce consistent results.

distractorExplanations.b: Pass scores are set in certification FAQs, not Standard 140.
distractorExplanations.c: Standard 140 is about software test suites.
distractorExplanations.d: Standard 140 supports software quality, not code adoption.

prompt: When utility data includes steam in MMBtu and electricity in kWh, normalization requires:

options.text: Converting all fuels to common units and separating base load from weather-dependent loads
Adding steam and kWh numerically without conversion
Discarding steam data if the model has no steam system
Using only the highest single hour of the year

explanation: Common units and regression help align model outputs with billed consumption patterns.

distractorExplanations.b: Different units cannot be summed without conversion.
distractorExplanations.c: Non-HVAC steam loads may still appear in meters and need allocation.
distractorExplanations.d: Normalization uses regression or degree-day methods on longer periods.

prompt: Assigning economizer high-limit shutoff in the model should follow:

options.text: Climate-appropriate outdoor air dry-bulb or enthalpy limits per code and design intent
Always disabling economizers in all climates
Setting economizer operation to zero outdoor air
Using heating setpoint as the economizer maximum

explanation: Correct economizer control limits affect cooling energy and ventilation hours.

distractorExplanations.b: Economizers are required or beneficial in many climates.
distractorExplanations.c: Economizers increase outdoor air for free cooling when conditions allow.
distractorExplanations.d: Economizer limits are based on outdoor conditions, not zone heating setpoints.

prompt: A modeling peer reviewer finds orientations rotated 90°. The impact is:

options.text: Solar gains and possibly economizer effectiveness are wrong, invalidating load and energy results
No impact because orientation does not affect loads
Only a cosmetic reporting issue
Corrected automatically by the weather file alone

explanation: Geometry QA must confirm building north rotation against architectural drawings.

distractorExplanations.b: Orientation strongly affects solar gains and shading.
distractorExplanations.c: Orientation errors are technical failures, not cosmetic.
distractorExplanations.d: Weather file does not fix building geometry orientation errors.

prompt: For multistory buildings with identical stacked floors, modelers often:

options.text: Use multipliers on representative floors to reduce geometry duplication while preserving total loads
Model only the ground floor and assume zero loads above
Delete all interior walls to speed solving
Apply a single occupant for the entire tower

explanation: Multipliers are acceptable when representative floors truly match stacked conditions.

distractorExplanations.b: Upper floors contribute significant loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Interior walls may affect zoning and solar distribution.
distractorExplanations.d: Occupancy scales with floor area and use type.

prompt: BEMP exam content at the analysis level may require candidates to:

options.text: Memorize only refrigerant GWP values without application
Recite ASHRAE staff names
Perform structural finite element analysis by hand
Interpret simulation output to recommend design or operational changes based on load disaggregation

explanation: ASHRAE certifications test recall, application, and analysis per ISO/IEC 17024 programs.

distractorExplanations.a: ASHRAE exams test application and analysis, not isolated memorization.
distractorExplanations.b: Exams focus on technical competency.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural FEA is outside BEMP scope.

prompt: Warehouse modeling differs from office modeling primarily in:

options.text: High bay volume, infiltration, minimal conditioning, and lighting/ventilation for storage vs occupancy patterns
Identical schedules and LPD as dense office space
No need to define any envelope properties
Mandatory district steam for all warehouses

explanation: Use-type drives ventilation codes, conditioning setpoints, and internal gains.

distractorExplanations.b: Warehouses have distinct use profiles and conditioning strategies.
distractorExplanations.c: All building types need envelope inputs.
distractorExplanations.d: HVAC systems vary; steam is not universal.

prompt: When exporting results for a code official, the modeler should provide:

options.text: Compliance forms, input summary, and evidence that Appendix G rules were followed
Only a verbal summary without files
Unrelated structural calculations
A blank simulation template

explanation: Jurisdictions require traceable compliance reports tied to recognized modeling standards.

distractorExplanations.b: Code compliance requires documented submittals.
distractorExplanations.c: Energy compliance submittals are separate from structural calcs.
distractorExplanations.d: Officials need completed compliance documentation.

prompt: Long-term persistence of modeled savings after retrofit depends most on:

options.text: Ongoing commissioning, operator training, and alarm response maintaining control sequences
Deleting trend logs to reduce server storage
Disabling all economizers after one year
Removing O&M manuals from the building

explanation: Operational drift is a leading cause of savings degradation after ECM implementation.

distractorExplanations.b: Trend data supports ongoing verification.
distractorExplanations.c: Disabling efficiency features erodes savings.
distractorExplanations.d: O&M documentation supports sustained performance.

prompt: In uncertainty reporting, CV(RMSE) and NMBE from Guideline 14 apply to:

options.text: Hourly or monthly comparisons between modeled and measured energy use
Only architectural door hardware schedules
Refrigerant cylinder color coding
Occupant parking assignments

explanation: NMBE measures bias; CV(RMSE) measures scatter between model and utility data.

distractorExplanations.b: CV(RMSE) and NMBE are statistical calibration metrics.
distractorExplanations.c: Refrigerant identification is unrelated to calibration statistics.
distractorExplanations.d: Parking assignments do not define calibration error metrics.
